title: Hypertension — Essential
oneLiner: Persistent BP ≥140/90 mmHg without identifiable secondary cause; major modifiable risk factor for CVD, stroke, and renal failure.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension):** BP >180/120 with acute target organ damage (TOD). **Single finding:** fundoscopy showing papilloedema and/or flame haemorrhages — implies arteriolar necrosis → encephalopathy, stroke, MI, AKI.  
- **Aortic dissection:** severe “tearing” chest/interscapular pain + BP asymmetry >20 mmHg between arms. **Single finding:** chest X-ray showing widened mediastinum (urgent CT aortogram).  
- **PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome):** headache, seizure, visual disturbances with severe hypertension. **Single finding:** new-onset seizure + BP >180/120 (urgent MRI brain).
sections.Presentation:
- Often asymptomatic – incidental finding on routine measurement / screening  
- Headache (occipital, early morning), dizziness, epistaxis, nocturia (due to pressure natriuresis)  
- Visual changes (blurring, scotomata), fatigue  
- **Exam:** BP measured correctly (seated, arm at heart level, cuff 2/3 upper arm, after 5 min rest) – check both arms. Fundoscopy (AV nipping, silver-wiring, haemorrhages, exudates, papilloedema). Peripheral pulses, bruits (carotid, abdominal, femoral). Signs of LVH (heaving apex beat, S4 gallop)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **White-coat hypertension:** elevated in clinic, normal home/ABPM – ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) or home BP monitoring (HBPM)  
- **Secondary hypertension (5–10%):**
  - Renovascular (renal artery stenosis) – young/female? diffuse atherosclerosis / flash pulmonary oedema; abdominal bruit  
  - Renal parenchymal disease – eGFR <60, proteinuria, abnormal urine dip  
  - Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s) – hypokalaemia (check after ACEi/ARB washout), raised aldosterone/renin ratio  
  - Phaeochromocytoma – paroxysmal hypertension, palpitations, sweating, headache; raised metanephrines  
  - Coarctation – brachial-femoral delay, radiofemoral delay, rib notching on CXR  
  - Obstructive sleep apnoea – obesity, snoring, daytime somnolence  
  - Drugs – NSAIDs, steroids, decongestants, oral contraceptives, liquorice  
- **Splitter:** Female <40 with hypokalaemia + high BP → check aldosterone/renin ratio (Conn’s); young thin male + abdominal bruit → renal artery duplex for fibromuscular dysplasia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Correct BP measurement (repeat ×3). Urine dipstick (protein, blood). ECG (LVH voltage criteria – e.g. Sokolow-Lyon >35 mm, strain pattern).  
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&Es, eGFR, fasting glucose, lipids, LFTs, TFTs (if clinical suspicion). Consider renin/aldosterone, metanephrines if secondary suspected.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: no routine imaging for Grade 1–2 hypertension  
  - If resistant / young / features of secondary: renal ultrasound + Doppler, CXR (coarctation, rib notching), echo (LVH, coarctation)  
  - CT/MRA renal arteries if RAS suspected; CT adrenals if Conn’s  
- **Special tests:** ABPM (gold standard for diagnosis – mean BP ≥135/85 mmHg daytime, ≥145/90 clinic equivalent). HBPM – average ≥135/85 mmHg.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Fundoscopy** in any patient with BP >180/120 or new-onset visual/neurological symptoms – papilloedema indicates hypertensive emergency requiring admission.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Hypertensive emergency (BP >180/120 + TOD):** admit to HDU/ICU – IV labetalol 50 mg bolus then infusion 2 mg/min, or IV nicardipine per local protocol. Also treat underlying cause (e.g. acute stroke – reduce BP cautiously by 15% in 1h).  
  - **Asymptomatic severe hypertension (BP >180/120, no TOD):** do NOT give IV therapy – start/increase oral agents and refer to outpatient clinic same day.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue oral medication. Start ACEi/ARB if not contraindicated – check U&Es before and after 1–2 weeks.  
  - Add CCB (amlodipine) or thiazide (indapamide) if BP remains >140/90.  
  - Treat any acute complications (stroke, ACS, renal impairment).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Lifestyle: salt <6 g/day, alcohol <14 units/week, BMI <25, exercise 30 min 5 days/week.  
  - Drug step-care (NICE):  
    - Age <55: A (ACEi e.g. ramipril 2.5 mg OD / ARB e.g. candesartan 8 mg OD)  
    - Age ≥55 or black African/Caribbean: C (CCB amlodipine 5 mg OD)  
    - Step 2: A + C  
    - Step 3: A + C + D (thiazide – indapamide 2.5 mg OD / chlortalidone)  
    - Resistant (on 3 drugs): A + C + D + spironolactone 12.5–25 mg OD (check K+ eGFR)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Observations: NEWS 6-hourly if stable, 1-2 hourly if hypertensive emergency  
  - VTE prophylaxis: dalteparin 5000 units SC OD if admitted for >24h (check weight, renal function)  
  - Diet: normal (low salt if tolerated)  
  - NBM only if surgery / unwell
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Ramipril** 2.5 mg OD – titrate up to 10 mg OD (max 10 mg). **CCF:** [check local policy] for lower starting dose.  
- **Candesartan** 8 mg OD (start 4 mg if volume depleted) – max 32 mg OD.  
- **Amlodipine** 5 mg OD – max 10 mg OD.  
- **Indapamide** 2.5 mg OD (modified release 1.5 mg). **Chlortalidone** 12.5–25 mg OD.  
- **Spironolactone** 12.5–25 mg OD – monitor K+ at 1 week, then monthly.  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - ACEi/ARB – avoid in pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia (K+ >5.5), severe AKI (eGFR <30 – use with caution).  
  - CCB – avoid verapamil with beta-blockers (bradycardia).  
  - Thiazides – avoid in gout, hyponatraemia.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - ACEi/ARB – reduce dose if eGFR <30 (start low, titrate).  
  - Amlodipine – no adjustment in renal impairment.  
  - Indapamide – avoid if eGFR <30 (thiazide ineffective).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **ACEi in bilateral renal artery stenosis** – can precipitate acute tubular necrosis (AKI). Always check U&Es/eGFR before first dose and after 2 weeks. Also: **IV labetalol** – risk of bradycardia / hypotension – monitor BP hourly.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Hypertensive encephalopathy (confusion, seizure, coma)  
  - Intracerebral / subarachnoid haemorrhage  
  - Acute STEMI / type 2 MI  
  - Aortic dissection  
  - Acute kidney injury (renal artery stenosis/cholesterol emboli)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Left ventricular hypertrophy → diastolic heart failure → HFpEF  
  - Ischaemic heart disease / myocardial infarction  
  - Ischaemic stroke / lacunar infarcts  
  - Chronic kidney disease (hypertensive nephrosclerosis)  
  - Hypertensive retinopathy → visual loss
sections.Prognosis:
- With sustained BP control (target <140/90, <130/80 if CKD/diabetes), CV risk reduced by ~30–40% (stroke > MI).  
- Untreated: 5-year survival ~70% if Grade 2–3 (accelarated CVD).  
- Hypertensive emergency: mortality ~10–15% even with treatment (high if renal failure, stroke).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check lying and standing BP in elderly** before escalating therapy – postural hypotension is common and adds fall risk.  
- **Start ACEi/ARB at night** (reduce first-dose hypotension) and **check U&Es at 1–2 weeks** – if K+ rises >5.5, stop and review.  
- **Use ABPM / HBPM** to confirm diagnosis – white-coat effect can lead to overtreatment.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT treat asymptomatic severe hypertension (e.g. 190/100) with IV therapy** – risk of iatrogenic stroke due to rapid BP drop (cerebral autoregulation loss).  
- **Do NOT miss secondary hypertension** – in young (<40), resistant, or with suggestive features – order appropriate tests before labelling essential.  
- **Do NOT forget pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age before starting ACEi/ARB – these drugs are teratogenic.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New severe headache, visual disturbance, chest pain, shortness of breath, or seizure  
  - Home BP >180/120 mmHg (any single reading) or consistently >160/100 despite meds  
  - New focal neurological symptoms (weakness, speech – consider TIA/stroke)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP within 1–2 weeks for BP check, U&Es, meds review  
  - Hypertension clinic referral if resistant / secondary suspected
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially in hypertensive emergency)  
  - BP >200/120 with any symptom: chest pain, dyspnoea, visual loss, seizure, GCS <15  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (possible end-organ malperfusion)  
  - GCS drop >2 points (hypertensive encephalopathy vs bleed)  
  - Suspected aortic dissection or acute stroke  
- **ICU/HDU referral:** hypertensive emergency requiring IV vasoactive therapy (labetalol, nicardipine) – BP reduction target: 15–25% in first hour, not normalisation.

rawMarkdown:
## Hypertension — Essential
**One-liner:** Persistent BP ≥140/90 mmHg without identifiable secondary cause; major modifiable risk factor for CVD, stroke, and renal failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension):** BP >180/120 with acute target organ damage (TOD). **Single finding:** fundoscopy showing papilloedema and/or flame haemorrhages — implies arteriolar necrosis → encephalopathy, stroke, MI, AKI.  
- **Aortic dissection:** severe “tearing” chest/interscapular pain + BP asymmetry >20 mmHg between arms. **Single finding:** chest X-ray showing widened mediastinum (urgent CT aortogram).  
- **PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome):** headache, seizure, visual disturbances with severe hypertension. **Single finding:** new-onset seizure + BP >180/120 (urgent MRI brain).

### Presentation
- Often asymptomatic – incidental finding on routine measurement / screening  
- Headache (occipital, early morning), dizziness, epistaxis, nocturia (due to pressure natriuresis)  
- Visual changes (blurring, scotomata), fatigue  
- **Exam:** BP measured correctly (seated, arm at heart level, cuff 2/3 upper arm, after 5 min rest) – check both arms. Fundoscopy (AV nipping, silver-wiring, haemorrhages, exudates, papilloedema). Peripheral pulses, bruits (carotid, abdominal, femoral). Signs of LVH (heaving apex beat, S4 gallop)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **White-coat hypertension:** elevated in clinic, normal home/ABPM – ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) or home BP monitoring (HBPM)  
- **Secondary hypertension (5–10%):**
  - Renovascular (renal artery stenosis) – young/female? diffuse atherosclerosis / flash pulmonary oedema; abdominal bruit  
  - Renal parenchymal disease – eGFR <60, proteinuria, abnormal urine dip  
  - Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s) – hypokalaemia (check after ACEi/ARB washout), raised aldosterone/renin ratio  
  - Phaeochromocytoma – paroxysmal hypertension, palpitations, sweating, headache; raised metanephrines  
  - Coarctation – brachial-femoral delay, radiofemoral delay, rib notching on CXR  
  - Obstructive sleep apnoea – obesity, snoring, daytime somnolence  
  - Drugs – NSAIDs, steroids, decongestants, oral contraceptives, liquorice  
- **Splitter:** Female <40 with hypokalaemia + high BP → check aldosterone/renin ratio (Conn’s); young thin male + abdominal bruit → renal artery duplex for fibromuscular dysplasia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Correct BP measurement (repeat ×3). Urine dipstick (protein, blood). ECG (LVH voltage criteria – e.g. Sokolow-Lyon >35 mm, strain pattern).  
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&Es, eGFR, fasting glucose, lipids, LFTs, TFTs (if clinical suspicion). Consider renin/aldosterone, metanephrines if secondary suspected.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: no routine imaging for Grade 1–2 hypertension  
  - If resistant / young / features of secondary: renal ultrasound + Doppler, CXR (coarctation, rib notching), echo (LVH, coarctation)  
  - CT/MRA renal arteries if RAS suspected; CT adrenals if Conn’s  
- **Special tests:** ABPM (gold standard for diagnosis – mean BP ≥135/85 mmHg daytime, ≥145/90 clinic equivalent). HBPM – average ≥135/85 mmHg.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Fundoscopy** in any patient with BP >180/120 or new-onset visual/neurological symptoms – papilloedema indicates hypertensive emergency requiring admission.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Hypertensive emergency (BP >180/120 + TOD):** admit to HDU/ICU – IV labetalol 50 mg bolus then infusion 2 mg/min, or IV nicardipine per local protocol. Also treat underlying cause (e.g. acute stroke – reduce BP cautiously by 15% in 1h).  
  - **Asymptomatic severe hypertension (BP >180/120, no TOD):** do NOT give IV therapy – start/increase oral agents and refer to outpatient clinic same day.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue oral medication. Start ACEi/ARB if not contraindicated – check U&Es before and after 1–2 weeks.  
  - Add CCB (amlodipine) or thiazide (indapamide) if BP remains >140/90.  
  - Treat any acute complications (stroke, ACS, renal impairment).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Lifestyle: salt <6 g/day, alcohol <14 units/week, BMI <25, exercise 30 min 5 days/week.  
  - Drug step-care (NICE):  
    - Age <55: A (ACEi e.g. ramipril 2.5 mg OD / ARB e.g. candesartan 8 mg OD)  
    - Age ≥55 or black African/Caribbean: C (CCB amlodipine 5 mg OD)  
    - Step 2: A + C  
    - Step 3: A + C + D (thiazide – indapamide 2.5 mg OD / chlortalidone)  
    - Resistant (on 3 drugs): A + C + D + spironolactone 12.5–25 mg OD (check K+ eGFR)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Observations: NEWS 6-hourly if stable, 1-2 hourly if hypertensive emergency  
  - VTE prophylaxis: dalteparin 5000 units SC OD if admitted for >24h (check weight, renal function)  
  - Diet: normal (low salt if tolerated)  
  - NBM only if surgery / unwell

### Drugs & Doses
- **Ramipril** 2.5 mg OD – titrate up to 10 mg OD (max 10 mg). **CCF:** [check local policy] for lower starting dose.  
- **Candesartan** 8 mg OD (start 4 mg if volume depleted) – max 32 mg OD.  
- **Amlodipine** 5 mg OD – max 10 mg OD.  
- **Indapamide** 2.5 mg OD (modified release 1.5 mg). **Chlortalidone** 12.5–25 mg OD.  
- **Spironolactone** 12.5–25 mg OD – monitor K+ at 1 week, then monthly.  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - ACEi/ARB – avoid in pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia (K+ >5.5), severe AKI (eGFR <30 – use with caution).  
  - CCB – avoid verapamil with beta-blockers (bradycardia).  
  - Thiazides – avoid in gout, hyponatraemia.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - ACEi/ARB – reduce dose if eGFR <30 (start low, titrate).  
  - Amlodipine – no adjustment in renal impairment.  
  - Indapamide – avoid if eGFR <30 (thiazide ineffective).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **ACEi in bilateral renal artery stenosis** – can precipitate acute tubular necrosis (AKI). Always check U&Es/eGFR before first dose and after 2 weeks. Also: **IV labetalol** – risk of bradycardia / hypotension – monitor BP hourly.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Hypertensive encephalopathy (confusion, seizure, coma)  
  - Intracerebral / subarachnoid haemorrhage  
  - Acute STEMI / type 2 MI  
  - Aortic dissection  
  - Acute kidney injury (renal artery stenosis/cholesterol emboli)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Left ventricular hypertrophy → diastolic heart failure → HFpEF  
  - Ischaemic heart disease / myocardial infarction  
  - Ischaemic stroke / lacunar infarcts  
  - Chronic kidney disease (hypertensive nephrosclerosis)  
  - Hypertensive retinopathy → visual loss  

### Prognosis
- With sustained BP control (target <140/90, <130/80 if CKD/diabetes), CV risk reduced by ~30–40% (stroke > MI).  
- Untreated: 5-year survival ~70% if Grade 2–3 (accelarated CVD).  
- Hypertensive emergency: mortality ~10–15% even with treatment (high if renal failure, stroke).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check lying and standing BP in elderly** before escalating therapy – postural hypotension is common and adds fall risk.  
- **Start ACEi/ARB at night** (reduce first-dose hypotension) and **check U&Es at 1–2 weeks** – if K+ rises >5.5, stop and review.  
- **Use ABPM / HBPM** to confirm diagnosis – white-coat effect can lead to overtreatment.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT treat asymptomatic severe hypertension (e.g. 190/100) with IV therapy** – risk of iatrogenic stroke due to rapid BP drop (cerebral autoregulation loss).  
- **Do NOT miss secondary hypertension** – in young (<40), resistant, or with suggestive features – order appropriate tests before labelling essential.  
- **Do NOT forget pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age before starting ACEi/ARB – these drugs are teratogenic.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New severe headache, visual disturbance, chest pain, shortness of breath, or seizure  
  - Home BP >180/120 mmHg (any single reading) or consistently >160/100 despite meds  
  - New focal neurological symptoms (weakness, speech – consider TIA/stroke)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP within 1–2 weeks for BP check, U&Es, meds review  
  - Hypertension clinic referral if resistant / secondary suspected

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially in hypertensive emergency)  
  - BP >200/120 with any symptom: chest pain, dyspnoea, visual loss, seizure, GCS <15  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (possible end-organ malperfusion)  
  - GCS drop >2 points (hypertensive encephalopathy vs bleed)  
  - Suspected aortic dissection or acute stroke  
- **ICU/HDU referral:** hypertensive emergency requiring IV vasoactive therapy (labetalol, nicardipine) – BP reduction target: 15–25% in first hour, not normalisation.
